days.headline: The queen stage of the Pyrenees to Gavarnie-Gèdre — over 4,100 m of climbing means the first full-blown GC showdown of the race.

picks.why: Sat level with the other GC favourites through stage 4, and sustained high-mountain finishes are historically his best terrain.
The most explosive climber in the race — a summit finish this hard is where he's most likely to attack from distance.
Leads the mountains classification through stage 4; a categorised summit day like this is built for him to add points.

days.headline: Pan-flat run to Bordeaux (just 701 m of climbing) — as close to a guaranteed bunch sprint as the Tour offers.

picks.why: The most conventional sprint finish of the week suits him perfectly.
Bordeaux's long finishing straights reward his big leadout train.
Leads the points classification through stage 4 and keeps scoring even when the pure flyers fade.

days.headline: Périgueux to Bergerac is gently rolling (1,353 m) — a sprint on paper, but the exposed Dordogne roads give a late attacker half a chance.

picks.why: Back-to-back sprint chances reward keeping him through the weekend.
Consistent top-5 threat and cheaper than the marquee sprinters if the run-in turns scrappy.
Strong enough to feature even on a day that doesn't come down to a clean field sprint.

days.headline: Malemort to Ussel packs 3,244 m of Massif Central chop on the eve of the rest day — a puncheur or breakaway day, not a sprint.

picks.why: Punchy, constantly up-and-down terrain is exactly his profile.
Thrives on repeated short climbs and hunts breakaways when the terrain gets choppy.
A relentless attacker on exactly this kind of constant up-and-down terrain.

days.headline: First stage back after the rest day, Aurillac to Le Lioran — steep, irregular Cantal climbs that suit an ambush more than a set-piece mountain train.

picks.why: A proven long-range attacker on medium mountain terrain like this.
A French climber who thrives in exactly this kind of irregular, ambush-friendly terrain.
Likely to go hunting stage wins and mountain points on days like this if he's out of the overall picture.

days.headline: A gentle run north along the Allier valley to Nevers — short by Tour standards and a likely bunch finish.

picks.why: A straightforward, fast finish is squarely in his wheelhouse.
Podium-regular sprinter who scores reliably on flat finishing days.
Another clean sprint chance for the strongest leadout train in the race.

fantasy.strategy: With the stage 1-7 transfer window closing after Bordeaux, use what's left of its 8 transfers to lock in a strong GC climber for the Gavarnie-Gèdre summit finish — the biggest single climbing haul of this horizon and the best place to spend a double-points bonus stage — while keeping a reliable sprinter aboard for stage 7's near-certain bunch gallop. Once the stage 8-14 window opens you get 8 fresh transfers: hold your sprinters through Bergerac, then rotate one out for a puncheur or breakaway rider ahead of the choppy Ussel stage and the ambush-prone climb to Le Lioran just after the rest day, before swinging back to a sprinter for Nevers. Keep the budget weighted toward climbers overall — climbing days outscore sprints, and this stretch of the race has more of them than flat ones.
